coredata.dc:title: A non-invasive wearable sweat biosensor with a flexible N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer for glucose monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yu-Chi
Rinawati, Mia
Chang, Ling-Yu
Wang, Yu-Xuan
Wu, Yu-Ting
Yen, Yi-Han
Chen, Kuan-Jung
Ho, Kuo-Chuan
Yeh, Min-Hsin
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the levels change in biomarkers over time, especially glucose, is crucial for diabetics to inform early therapy. Recently, wearable sweat biosensors which operate in a non-invasive way have raised attention, providing continuous monitoring of the severity level. Nevertheless, the key challenges are that multiplexed motions may result in undesirable metal cracking by the rigid electrocatalytic layer, leading to decreased sensitivity and stability. In this work, we designed an N-GQDs anchored PANI matrix to realize flexible wearable biosensors with high detection accuracy. Upon the enhanced electron transfer by N-GQDs, N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite offers greater sensitivity in H2O2 detection compared to pristine PANI, resulting in a sensitivity of 68.1 ± 1.11 and 44.06 ± 2.1 μA mM−1 cm−2, respectively. Moreover, after the glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilization, GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based biosensors had shown excellent performance for glucose detection in artificial sweat. Precise glucose detection was also maintained after integrating into a flexible electrode, the sensitivity of the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based biosensor had retained 93.2 % with no apparent cracks in the morphology of the nanocomposite layer, compared to GOx/Pt-based one (71.3 %) toward glucose detection after a continuous bending test. Thus, the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer can provide reliable long-term monitoring with robust electrodes for non-invasive human sweat glucose monitoring on a wearable biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzymatic
Glucose
Graphene quantum dots
Polyaniline
Sweat
Wearable biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and N-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs)
Preparation of PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI films on the SPCE
Electrochemical measurement of PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs
Electrocatalytic activities for N-GQDs/PANI toward H2O2 reduction
Electrocatalytic activities of various N doping level N-GQDs modified PANI toward H2O2 reduction
Electrocatalytic activities for GOx/N-GQDs/PANI based biosensor toward glucose detection
GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based Wearable biosensor for glucose monitoring in artificial sweat

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology
Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2 , 111-2221-E-011-011 , 110-2623-E-606-001 , and 110-3116-F-011-004 ) and industry-academia cooperation project (NTUST-Mighty Electronic-No. 9967) We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of MOST (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2, 111-2221-E-011-011, 110-2623-E-606-001, and 110-3116-F-011-004) and industry-academia cooperation project (NTUST-Mighty Electronic-No. 9967) We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of MOST (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N-GQDs, inset: the size distribution of N-GQDs; (b) XPS survey scan of N-GQDs; (c) the Raman spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs; (d) PL excitation-emission mapping of N-GQDs, inset: light emission of N-GQDs under UV excitation at 365 nm.
The morphology of (a) PANI/SPCEs, and (b) N-GQDs/PANI/SPCEs; (c) FTIR of PANI and N-GQDs/PANI; (d) Raman spectra of PANI and N-GQDs/PANI.

(a) Amperometry curve, and (b) the corresponding sensitivity of SPCEs with pristine PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI for a linear range of 0–1 mM H2O2 ; (c) the double layer capacitance, and (d) Nyquist of SPCEs with pristine PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI in the presence of 1 mM H2O2 in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2) with −0.75 V (V vs. Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl) as applied potential.

(a) Amperometry curve, and (b) the corresponding sensitivity of PANI and N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs with a linear range of 0–1 mM M H2O2; (c) the electron transfer rate of PANI and N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs with a linear range of 0.1 mM H2O2 in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2); (d) the correlation curve between the pyridinic N content of N-GQDs and charge transfer resistance.

(a) Sensitivity of GOx/PANI and GOx/N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs with a linear range of 0–0.5 mM glucose for glucose detection in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2); GOx/N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs for glucose detection: (b) the corresponding interference test with the presence of glucose, uric acid, urea, ascorbic acid, and lactate in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2); (c) the long-term operation test, (d) the repeatability test on 5 individual running, and (e) the long-term stability test with 0.5 mM glucose in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2).

(a) Scheme of the flexible electrode for wearable sweat biosensor; (b) amperometry curve of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI/ flexible electrode with the linear range of 0–0.3 mM glucose in artificial sweat (pH=5.0); (c) scheme of the wearable sweat biosensor in the different bending states; (d) sensitivity comparison of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI and GOx/Pt/C flexible electrode with a linear range of 0–0.15 mM glucose under different bending states in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2).
Schematic illustration of non-invasive wearable sweat biosensor with a flexible N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer.

head.ce:title: A non-invasive wearable sweat biosensor with a flexible N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer for glucose monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei 10607, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the levels change in biomarkers over time, especially glucose, is crucial for diabetics to inform early therapy. Recently, wearable sweat biosensors which operate in a non-invasive way have raised attention, providing continuous monitoring of the severity level. Nevertheless, the key challenges are that multiplexed motions may result in undesirable metal cracking by the rigid electrocatalytic layer, leading to decreased sensitivity and stability. In this work, we designed an N-GQDs anchored PANI matrix to realize flexible wearable biosensors with high detection accuracy. Upon the enhanced electron transfer by N-GQDs, N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite offers greater sensitivity in H2O2 detection compared to pristine PANI, resulting in a sensitivity of 68.1 ± 1.11 and 44.06 ± 2.1 μA mM−1 cm−2, respectively. Moreover, after the glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilization, GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based biosensors had shown excellent performance for glucose detection in artificial sweat. Precise glucose detection was also maintained after integrating into a flexible electrode, the sensitivity of the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based biosensor had retained 93.2 % with no apparent cracks in the morphology of the nanocomposite layer, compared to GOx/Pt-based one (71.3 %) toward glucose detection after a continuous bending test. Thus, the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer can provide reliable long-term monitoring with robust electrodes for non-invasive human sweat glucose monitoring on a wearable biosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the continual fluctuation of biomarker chemical signals, complex patterns of metabolites, hormones, and inflammatory changing are generated throughout a variety of periods ranging from hourly to daily. Regulating and maintaining biomarkers, and continuous evaluation within the body are profoundly essential to rise the survival rate of the living organism and its implications for the development of disease diagnoses and therapies. Wearable biosensors have drawn tremendous attention for on-body monitoring of a wide range of vital indicators for health, fitness, and biomedical applications. In contrast to current biosensors, they offer distinct advantages for real-time, online, and non-invasive health monitoring [1,2]. Rapid wearable technology for non-invasive monitoring, such as sweat, tears, etc, has evolved over the years ever since the prevalent use of smartphones and mobile electronics. Wearable biosensors based on sweat analysis offers a non-invasive way to monitor biomarker, enabling viable options beyond blood. Sweat contains a substantial concentration of analytes, (such as glucose, lactate, uric acid, and urea) ideal for noninvasive monitoring fluid [3–6].

Among biomarkers, glucose is a key indicator of hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia-related diseases, as such diabetes, Cushing syndrome, and Galactosemia. In contrast to existing commercial glucose monitors which require invasive blood sampling, a non-invasive glucose sensor based on sweat has recently gained interest. Typical glucose concentration ranged from 0.2 to 0.6 mM within the sweat, therefore, it is highly sensitive to detect tiny glucose fluctuations [7]. Ever since the non-enzymatic glucose sensor was developed, the amperometric enzymatic glucose sensor (i.e glucose oxidase (GOx)-based) continues to lead in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility [8–10]. Although sweat is easily accessible, low excretion rates with low glucose concentrations are the main challenges. Thus, designing wearable devices, such as patches on human skin that enable continuous detection of low glucose concentrations is crucial. However, establishing a wearable device can be challenging when noticeable deformations occur, creating cracks and damaging the fabricated electrodes, affecting the electrochemical properties [11,12].

Addressing those issues, developing flexible electrodes while maintaining stability and flexibility is critical. Another key issue is the immobilization of the GOx, which require a proper matrix to attach. To date, some of the conducting polymers, such as Polypyrrole (PPy), polyaniline (PANI), and poly(3,4-ethyldioxythiophene) (PEDOT) have been studied for flexible electrodes, as well as serving as a matrix for GOx immobilization [13]. However, several issues still existed, such as Polypyrrole's thermal stability and limited yield [14], as well as the water insolubility of PANI [15]. As a consequence, selecting a conducting polymer with excellent stability is essential. PANI, a benzoid ring with a single and double C-C bond sequences conductive polymer, has gotten much interest due to its stability, flexibility, and biocompatibility. Yet, PANI-based biosensors might have a limited detection range and poor catalytic activity inside the neutral electrolyte, which is the actual pH of the body fluid [16]. Nowadays, to tackle these issues, introducing ternary and quaternary nanocomposite with carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene quantum dot, and reduced graphene oxide is needed due to its excellent conductivity, high chemical stability, and large surface area [17].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional graphene fragments with a size of less than 20 nm, are well known for their outstanding electrical conductivity and high specific surface area, making them a promising candidate for biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and solar cells [18–21]. GQDs featured an abundance of edge and defect sites, which is advantageous for hosting catalytic reactions, such as enzymatic glucose reactions. Doping heteroatoms such as N, P, B, F, and S will substantially disorient the electron network due to the high electronegativity difference between carbon and the dopant, leading to the charge distribution, thus, improving the conductivity [22–25]. Exceptional charge transportation and tunable electronic features of heteroatom-doped GQDs may be able to further boosting the conductivity of PANI in neutral electrolytes.

In light of the working mechanism of those pioneering works, herein, we design a flexible glucose biosensor in human sweat composed of a GOx/N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer. PANI was selected as the matrix owing to its flexibility and excellent attachment for GOx, where N-GQDs supplied copious N-rich functional groups that act as electron transfer agents. This, will address the electrical conductivity issues inside neutral electrolytes, enhancing the electroactivity toward glucose detection. The N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer was realized by one-step electropolymerization technique. The enhanced sensitivity and selectivity were observed, and excellent stability was maintained from the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer, providing future insight for the wearable biosensor development which provided the non-invasive and reliable long-term glucose monitoring, as shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and N-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 40 mg of citric acid was utterly dissolved in 40 mL DI water with an ultrasonication, then placed to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven for three hours at 200 °C. The light yellow GQDs solution was dialyzed against DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab. Inc, 3000 Da) to remove contaminants. For the preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with different doping levels, the various amounts of ammonium hydroxide (3, 5, 8 and 10 mL) were added to the 40 mL of citric acid aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1). After wholly dissolved, the similar hydrothermal treatment was applied, then dialyzed to remove unreacted compounds, designated as N-GQDs-x (x = 3, 5, 8, and 10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI films on the SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI films, the screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) with polyethylene terephthalate (PET) were used as the matrix. The plating solution consisted of 1.0 M aniline monomer, 2.0 M HCl, and DI water in an aqueous solution. During GQDs/PANI or N-GQDs/PANI plating solution, DI water will be replaced with GQDs or N-GQDs solutions. A three-electrode setup was set up with a 0.52 cm2 of SPCE working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (3.0 M) reference electrode, and a Pt foil counter electrode. Following that, as-obtained nanocomposite films deposited by applying 0.1 mA to the SPCE at the charge densities of 150 mC cm−2. Then, rinsed with 0.1 M HCl after electropolymerization to remove any unattached aniline monomer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurement of PANI, GQDs/PANI, and N-GQDs/PANI based SPCEs
ce:section.ce:para: A conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell was used for the cyclic voltammetry, with a modified SPCE participating as the working electrode (WE), a Pt foil acting as the counter electrode (CE), and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) acting as the reference electrode (RE). When injecting 0–1 mM H2O2 into PANI-based electrodes during amperometric measurement within 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2), the chronoamperometric response for the modified electrode was observed. The electroactivity toward glucose detection was measured by chronoamperometry with a concentration region of 0–0.5 mM glucose. The long-term stability of N-GQDs/PANI modified SPCEs were assessed every three days. The glucose detection inside artificial sweat was measured by chronoamperometry with the concentration range of 0–0.35 mM glucose. For the bending test, the flexible electrode was placed on the prosthetic hand and bent at 10° with the assistance of the linear motor system with the parameters of 0.5 m/s2 of acceleration and deceleration. The wait time of rise position and fall position were set at 5 s, and 2 mm distance difference between rising and fall positions.

N-GQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal process with citric acid and ammonia hydroxide as precursors. TEM has been used to investigate the morphology of the as-synthesized N-GQDs ( Fig. 1a) and the histogram-relative insets showing the particle size distribution of N-GQDs, where the diameters of the N-GQDs are in the range of 2.5–4.5 nm with an average of 3.5 ± 0.38 nm. The XPS survey scan depicted in Fig. 1b, which gives the existence of the N 1 s (401 eV) peak in N-GQDs, along with the presence of the C 1 s (285 eV) and O 1 s (533 eV) peaks, verifies the doping of N atoms.

As revealed in Fig. 1 c, Raman spectroscopy was performed to investigate the GQD’s disorder and crystallinity disorder by N doping. The CC vibration of sp2 carbon causes the distinctive G band at 1600 cm−1 in GQDs. A noticeable G band peak shifting is seen after the N dopant to the graphitic system, by which N-GQDs’ characteristic is 1589 cm−1. N doping may contribute to inducing tensile strain in the graphene lattice [26]. While the D band is related to graphene’s defect-induced [27], the N-GQDs possessed a wider D band, clarifying the abundant disordered sp2 carbon by N intercalation [28]. Subsequently, the degree of disorder (measured by the intensity ratios, ID/IG), of N-GQD’s increased from 0.54 to 0.98, indicating that the N doping created significant defects, that play role in enhancing electrocatalytic activity.

Excitation-emission-intensity spectra can be used to define the distinctive photoluminescence (PL) activity of N-GQDs as illustrated in Fig. 1d, and GQDs as a comparison (Fig. S1). The GQDs showed an excitation wavelength at 330 nm and strong emission at 400 nm, while N-GQDs showed strong emissions around 440 nm with an excitation wavelength at 380 nm. The red-shift excitation wavelength might be caused by electron transfer between the unpaired electrons of the N functional groups [29,30]. The electron donation from the lone pair electrons of N functional groups to the GQDs’ benzene ring, induces charge redistribution [31]. Moreover, the higher PL intensity of N-GQDs could have been contributed by the additional defects upon N incorporation [32], confirming the successful synthesis. XPS has been performed to evaluate the N doping (See Supporting Information, Fig. S3 and the details). In agreement with the redshift of the PL excitation spectra for N-GQDs, the addition of pyridinic and graphitic N can probably boost the electronic charge in N-GQDs [33].

The morphology of pristine PANI and N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposites was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. The pristine PANI displayed an irregular structure with a thickness around 0.16 µm (Fig. S4), while the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer exhibited a porous nanorod-like. To study the functionalization activity of N-GQDs and PANI, the pristine PANI and N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The spectrum of pristine PANI reveals a distinct peak at 1597 cm−1, which contributed to the CC vibration [34]. The absorption peaks at 1558 cm−1 and 1483 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration of the quinoid (N = Q=N) and the benzene (N-B-N) [35]. A characteristic band at 1290 cm−1 is the aliphatic amine of C-N vibration [36]. The C-C stretching and C-H in a plane on the 1,2,4-ring are attributed to the bands at 1082 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1, respectively [37,38]. Interestingly, the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite are mostly identical with pristine PANI, except for a noticeable additional peak at 1105 cm-1 of N-GQDs' C-O-C bond, which verifies the existence of N-GQDs in the PANI matrix [39,40]. Furthermore, Raman spectra of pristine PANI and N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite (Fig. 2d) revealed a shift at 1161 cm−1 that was attributed to the in-plane C-H bending in the quinoid of PANI. The weak C-N stretching mode is also observed at 1213 cm−1. The Raman shifts at 1460 and 1338 cm−1 were contributed by the CN- and C-N- stretching in the quinoid and benzenoid rings, respectively [41]. Surprisingly, the electrostatic interaction between PANI chains and N-GQDs caused substantial Raman shifts from 1368 to 1375 cm−1 and from 1581 to 1585 cm−1 which correspond to C-C and CC stretching of N-GQDs/PANI [41–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities for N-GQDs/PANI toward H2O2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: Before assessing the glucose detection with GOx, the catalytic activity of the pristine PANI and N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite for H2O2 detection in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2) had been carried out by cyclic voltammogram (CV), as shown in Fig. S5a and b, respectively. The corresponding redox peak at − 0.75 V is attributed to the conversion of the leucoemeraldine (LE) to emeraldine (EM) form of PANI. For such a negative potential is attributed by the limited conductivity of PANI inside the pH-neutral medium. In the region, another redox peak in the higher potential was corresponding to the transformation between the EM and pernigraniline (PE) form of PANI [44]. In the presence of H2O2, the LE will turn into EM in the structure of PANI while the H2O2 will reduce into H2O [45]. The significant current increase was noticeable after introducing N-GQDs, suggesting that the electrocatalytic activities of PANI toward H2O2 reduction could be facilitated by modifying N-GQDs. To evaluate the electrochemical activities of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI/SPCEs for glucose detection, the amperometric response of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI/SPCEs with various concentration of glucose (0.05−0.5 mM) were measured and then recorded with − 0.75 V as an applied potential as shown in the Fig. S6. Noticeable current increases at − 0.75 V were observed as the glucose concentration increased, implying the major role of N-GQDs to the electrocatalytic activities of PANI toward glucose detection. The amperometric response of pristine PANI and N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs with various concentrations of the H2O2 (0–1 mM) with an applied potential of − 0.75 V (vs. 3 M Ag/AgCl) was shown in Fig. 3a, with GQDs/PANI based SPCEs as a comparison to confirm the N-doping effect. As expected, the N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs exhibit the highest amperometric response. Fig. 3b illustrates the corresponding linear plots to assess the sensitivity and correlated R-squared values, which revealed that N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs exhibited an H2O2 concentration dependence behavior with a promising linear response throughout 0–1 mM H2O2. The results also showed better sensitivity of the SPCEs with N-GQDs/PANI, valuing 56.4 ± 1.59 μA mM−1 cm−2 (R2 =0.992) compared with PANI-based SPCEs (44.1 ± 2.1 μA mM−1 cm−2, R2 =0.978) and GQDs/PANI based SPCEs (47.6 ± 0.94 μA mM−1 cm−2, R2 =0.996), indicating that modified the PANI with N-GQDs can achieve the greater catalytic activity of H2O2 detection.

To investigate the exposed active sites, the double-layer capacitances (Cdl) were utilized to reflect the relative electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the electrocatalyst, presented in Fig. 3c. The corresponding SPCEs Cdl value was performed by CV at various scan rates in 0.01 M PBS solution with 1 mM H2O2 (Fig. S7a-c). N-GQDs/PANI SPCEs exhibited the highest value of Cdl of 1.43 mF cm−2, followed by GQDs/PANI (1.16 mF cm−2) and pristine PANI (0.627 mF cm−2). This verifies that the heteroatom-doped had a major role in creating defective sites, as confirmed by the Raman and XPS results. The Nyquist plot of corresponding SPCEs was further revealed by EIS measurement, as shown in Fig. 3d. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of corresponding SPCEs representing the reaction process appeared in the high-to-medium frequency region of the impedance plot, followed by a line representing ion diffusion in the electrode material in the low-frequency zone [46]. The result shows that pristine PANI SPCEs possess the largest Rct value of 179 Ω, in line with the poor intrinsic conductivity of PANI under the higher pH solution [44]. Surprisingly, the value of Rct of SPCEs with N-GQDs/PANI had dramatically decreased (141.7 Ω), which confirmed that the N-GQDs had improved the interfacial electron transportation [47]. In neutral solutions, attaching negatively charged anions to the PANI will attract positively charged protons to further improve the conductivity of PANI [44]. Therefore, the N-GQDs can successfully facilitate the overall charge transfer of the PANI matrix to further boost H2O2 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities of various N doping level N-GQDs modified PANI toward H2O2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the correlation of N doping for H2O2 reduction reaction, the electrochemical performance of N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposites with various N doping levels were studied. The amperometric response with 1 mM of H2O2 at an applied potential of − 0.75 V (vs. Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl) was shown in Fig. 4a, and the corresponding chronoamperometric responses (Fig. S8a and b). N-GQDs-17/PANI modified SPCEs possess the highest reduction current densities response and sensitivity of 68.1 ± 1.11 μA mM−1 cm−2 (R2 =0.997) compared with other N-GQDs/PANI/SPCEs. The results revealed that varying the N doping amount in N-GQDs could affect the electroactivity toward H2O2 reduction. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of PANI was modified with the diverse N content shown in Table S2.

Pyridinic N in the carbon was known to alter the electron-withdrawing nature of the carbon basal plane [48]. The lowest Rct values of the N-GQDs/PANI (Fig. 4d, S9) were indicating that the pyridinic N can reduce the inner electrical resistance of N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite for transporting more free electrons and proton [49]. Additionally, pyridinic N in the carbon matrix was crucial in providing pseudocapacitance characteristics by introducing negative charges to the carbon surface (inset of Fig. 4d), increasing the conductivity of PANI in a neutral environment [50]. As a result, modifying the PANI with N-GQDs can improve the electrocatalytic behavior of PANI in a neutral medium.

In the practical use of electrochemical sensors, dissolved oxygen reduction always occurs in most electrodes, limiting the accuracy of H2O2 detection [51]. To select the suitable applied potential for H2O2 detection without interfering with oxygen reduction, the CVs of corresponding sensors were measured in the air-saturated, O2-saturated, and O2-free 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2), as shown in Fig. S10a. Surprisingly, no significant difference in the reduction currents for the N-GQDs/PANI-based sensor under all of the conditions, indicating that the dissolved oxygen reduction could not influence H2O2 detection. The corresponding sensitivity was also illustrated in Fig. S9b, where no significant difference was also observed for the N2-saturated and air-saturated conditions, confirming the reliability of N-GQDs/PANI-based sensor towards the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 in the dissolved oxygen-containing condition. In the O2-saturated condition, the sensitivity of the N-GQDs/PANI-based sensor had slightly decreased, which may be attributed to the attached dissolved oxygen on the surface of the electrode that suppress the H2O2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities for GOx/N-GQDs/PANI based biosensor toward glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs was investigated by CV with various concentrations of glucose (0.05–0.5 mM) in 0.01 M PBS (pH=7.2), as presented in Fig. S11a. The reduction current of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs was gradually increased with the increased glucose concentration, indicating the ability to detect the glucose concentration difference. The corresponding amperometric response, sensitivity and correlation R squared values were shown in Fig. S11b–c. According to Fig. 5a, GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs exhibited a higher sensitivity of 28.2 ± 0.62 μA mM−1cm−2 (R2 =0.995) with the limit of detection of 0.034 mM, compared to the GOx/PANI based SPCEs (19.7 ± 0.46 μA mM−1 cm−2, R2 =0.994, LOD =0.045 mM), which indicate that N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite had the excellent electrocatalytic ability to facilitate glucose detection. The interference test was conducted using several common chemical substances that coexist in human sweat (such as uric acid, urea, ascorbic acid, and lactate) as well as to further assess the selectivity of GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs. The interference test responses collected in the Fig. 5b, demonstrated a distinct response to the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs after the addition of 0.3 mM glucose, while only minor fluctuations were noticed after the subsequent additions of 0.02 mM uric acid, 0.025 mM ascorbic acid, 0.075 mM urea, and 5 mM lactate. This result indicates that N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs possess remarkable selectivity toward glucose detection due to the selective oxidation of immobilized GOx in N-GQDs/PANI, despite the interference bias in human sweat.

The stability and reproducibility of the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCEs were evaluated to broaden the possible applications of non-invasive biosensors, depicted in Fig. 5c, where it shows negligible current decline while glucose concentrations are continuously monitored at 0.5 mM. The corresponding response was stable and reliable with retention of 95.7 % after 8.500 s of continuous measurement. The reproducibility test for 5 times individual running tests of the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based SPCE was conducted and displayed in Fig. 5d, where the sensitivity remained consistent at 22.4 μAmM−1cm−2 (R2 =0.972), identical with the initial individual sample (26.7 μAmM−1cm−2, R2 =0.997). It revealed outstanding reproducibility with promising accuracy several times. Moreover, it also exhibited extraordinary stability with 89.4 % retention after 30 days with storage conditions of 4 °C (Fig. 5e), which provided an endless opportunity to introduce N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite in various types of electrochemical glucose biosensors with excellent sensitivity and durability. Compared to the other PANI-based enzymatic glucose sensors (Table S3), the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer had shown outstanding sensitivity, the limit of detection (LOD), and durability toward glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based Wearable biosensor for glucose monitoring in artificial sweat
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of a GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based electrode for monitoring glucose concentration from human sweat by wearable biosensing platform, a GOx/N-GQDs/PANI modified electrode with the PET-based soft-flexible sensor was fabricated. The conception of a non-invasive wearable biosensor is schematically illustrated in Fig. 6a, which provides glucose measurement in human sweat by an alternative non-invasively method. Remarkably, the time sequence diagram in Fig. 6b and Fig. S12 manifested that GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based flexible electrode could reflect the linear response of current density as the variation of glucose in time with the fluctuation of glucose concentration inside artificial sweat, suggesting its considerable potential for continuous glucose monitoring from human sweat in the future. To investigate its flexibility, the corresponding electrode was attached to the prosthetic hand to mimic real human motion. The linear motor was utilized to bend the assembled electrodes to simulate the trivial movements of the wrist, including GOx/Pt/C based electrode (Fig. 6c). The performances of both flexible electrodes were investigated by the amperometry and calibration plot to assess sensitivity with the mechanical bend in the presence of 0.050–0.15 mM glucose (Fig. S14a-b). Fig. 6d and Fig. S14c cleared that GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based flexible electrodes retained 93.2 % (56.4 μA mM−1 cm−2) sensitivity of its initial value (60.5 μA mM−1 cm−2), while the GOx/Pt/C based flexible electrodes had dramatically reduced with 29.7 % retention. To flexibility assessment of electrodes before and after the bending test showed other N-GQDs/PANI remained (Fig. S14a-d). Therefore, it is fair to say that the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite is a promising electrode material for flexible biosensors.

In conclusion, the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer had been successful to realize a wearable sweat biosensor for glucose monitoring with promising sensitivity and outstanding flexibility. Despite the lower electroactivity and conductivity in the neutral electrolyte, PANI has been commonly utilized for wearable biosensors due to its flexibility. To address them, N-GQDs were introduced as electron transfer agents due to their abundant N-rich functional groups. Our results showed that the N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite successfully lowered the charge transfer resistance of PANI, resulting in the enhanced sensitivity of N-GQDs/PANI toward H2O2 detection. Since the N dopant of N-GQDs acted a critical role in lowering the charge transfer resistance of PANI, it resulted in a high sensitivity of 68.1 ± 1.11 μA mM−1 cm−2 toward H2O2 detection. Notably, the GOx/N-GQDs/PANI modified SPCEs have demonstrated remarkable sensitivity (28.2 ± 0.62 μA mM−1 cm−2), selectivity, repeatability (5 times), and 89.4 % long-term stability test for 30 days, providing the limitless opportunity to utilize GOx/N-GQDs/PANI nanocomposite layer in different types of glucose electrochemical sensors with promising sensitivity and stability. The as-fabricated GOx/N-GQDs/PANI-based flexible electrode also exhibited excellent flexibility during mechanical bending, demonstrating promising applications in real-world wearable biological diagnostics.
The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.
The supporting information contains figures regarding the characterization of the materials.

Yu-Chi Lin: synthesized and characterized material, performed electrochemical analysis. Mia Rinawati: drafted the manuscript, graphed and analyzed the data. Ling-Yu Chang: Analyzed the data and revised the manuscript. Yu-Xuan Wang: performed the long-term stability test and interference test. Yu-Ting Wu: Material characterization and data analysis. Yi-Han Yen: designed the experiments for long-term stability test under well-controlled conditions. Kuan Jung Chen: design and fabricated the SPCE soft biosensor. Kuo-Chuan Ho: conceived the idea and revised the paper. Min-Hsin Yeh: conceived the idea, designed the experiments, analyzed the data, discussed the results, wrote and revised the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan (MOST 109-2221-E-011-061-MY2, 111-2221-E-011-011, 110-2623-E-606-001, and 110-3116-F-011-004) and industry-academia cooperation project (NTUST-Mighty Electronic-No. 9967) We also thank Ms. C.-Y. Chien of MOST (located at National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133617.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Yu-Chi Lin received his MS degree in the Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh in 2022. His research interests mainly focus on designing electrocatalysts for stretchable electrochemical glucose biosensors.

Ms. Mia Rinawati is currently a Ph.D. student in the Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh. Her research interest mainly focus on the design of noble metal free electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER).

Dr. Ling-Yu Chang received her Ph.D. degree in the Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering at the National Taiwan University (NTU) under the supervision of Prof. Jiang-Jen Lin in 2013. After that, she performed as a postdoctoral fellowship in the Prof. Jiang-Jen Lin research group from 2013 to 2014. Her research interests mainly focus on functional polymers design for dye-sensitized solar cells. Currently, she works in LCY chemical Corp. for market analysis and product marketing since 2014.

Ms. Yu-Xuan Wang received his MS degree in the Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh in 2021. Her research interests mainly focus on designing graphene quantum dots based electrocatalysts for electrochemical sensors.

Ms. Yu-Ting Wu received his MS degree in the Department of Chemical Engineering at National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech), Taiwan, under the supervision of Prof. Min-Hsin Yeh in 2021. Her research interests mainly focus on designing noble metal-free electrocatalysts for dye-sensitized solar cells and electrochemical sensors.

Dr. Kuan Jung Chen received his Ph.D. degree in the Department of Chemical Engineering from the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech) under the supervision of Prof. Bing Joe Hwang in 2012. His research expertise was electrochemical sensor design and development, nanomaterial design and synthesis, biosensor design and development. Currently, he works in Mighty Electronic Co, Ltd.

Dr. Kuo-Chuan Ho received his BS and MS degrees in Department of Chemical Engineering from National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan in 1978 and 1980, respectively. He received his PhD in Chemical Engineering at the University of Rochester, USA in 1986. Currently he is a Distinguished Professor jointly appointed by the Department of Chemical Engineering and Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering at National Taiwan University. His research interests mainly surround applications of chemically modified electrodes to sensing and electro-optical devices, including dye-sensitized solar cells and electrochromic devices.

Dr. Min-Hsin Yeh received his B. Sc. and M. Sc. degrees in Department of Chemical Engineering from National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (Taiwan Tech) in 2007 and 2009, respectively, and Ph.D. degree in Department of Chemical Engineering from National Taiwan University (NTU) under the supervision of Prof. Kuo-Chuan Ho in 2013. He then performed as a postdoctoral research fellow at the School of Materials Science and Engineering in Georgia Institute of Technology with Prof. Zhong Lin Wang’s research group (2014–2016). Currently, he is an Assistant Professor in Department of Chemical Engineering of National Taiwan University of Science & Technolohy (Taiwan Tech) since 2018. His research interests are focused in the area of the nanomaterials and their applications in electrochemistry, photoelectrochemistry, energy harvesting systems, energy storage devices, and self-powered systems. He is also interested in the development and application of synchrotron radiation techniques and in-situ analytical techniques in nanomaterials study. He has published over 90 peer-reviewed papers (h-index=42; Citations ∼6700) in high impact journals such as Sci. Adv., Adv. Mater., Adv. Energy Mater., Adv. Fun. Mater., ACS Nano, Nano Energy, etc.
